epigraph.text: He who is faithful in a very little is also faithful in much.
epigraph.quote: Conformal maps preserve the shape of the infinitely small.

content.text:
A conformal map preserves angles. This seemingly simple property has 
profound geometric and physical consequences.

content.title: Conformal Map
content.content:
A function $f: D \to \C$ is **conformal** at 
$z_0 \in D$ if $f$ preserves angles between curves at $z_0$. That is, 
if two smooth curves through $z_0$ meet at angle $\theta$, their images 
under $f$ meet at the same angle $\theta$ (in both magnitude and orientation).

We say $f$ is conformal on $D$ if it is conformal at every point of $D$.

content.title: Analytic Functions are Conformal
content.content:
Let $f$ be analytic on a domain $D$. Then $f$ is conformal at every 
point $z_0$ where $f'(z_0) \neq 0$.
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
If $\gamma(t)$ is a smooth curve with $\gamma(0) = z_0$ and 
$\gamma'(0) \neq 0$, then near $t = 0$:
\[
 f(\gamma(t)) \approx f(z_0) + f'(z_0)\gamma'(0) \cdot t.
\]
The tangent to the image curve at $f(z_0)$ is $f'(z_0)\gamma'(0)$.

Multiplication by $f'(z_0) = |f'(z_0)|e^{i\alpha}$ rotates by $\alpha$ 
and scales by $|f'(z_0)|$. Since rotation is applied to all tangent 
vectors equally, angles between curves are preserved.

content.title: The squaring function
content.problem:
$f(z) = z^2$ is conformal except at $z = 0$ (where $f'(0) = 0$).

At the origin, angles are *doubled*: a ray at angle $\theta$ maps 
to a ray at angle $2\theta$. The function is not conformal there.

Away from the origin, $z^2$ maps the first quadrant to the upper half-plane, 
preserving angles at every point.

content.title: The exponential
content.problem:
$f(z) = e^z$ is conformal everywhere (since $e^z \neq 0$).

It maps horizontal lines to rays from the origin and vertical lines to 
circles centered at the origin. The angles between these families (all 
$90°$) are preserved.

content.title: Local Structure of Analytic Functions
content.content:
Let $f$ be analytic at $z_0$ with $f'(z_0) = \cdots = f^{(n-1)}(z_0) = 0$ 
but $f^{(n)}(z_0) \neq 0$ (where $n \geq 1$). Then near $z_0$:

1. Angles are multiplied by $n$.

2. $f$ is locally $n$-to-$1$ (except at $z_0$ itself).
content.label: Theorem

content.text:
This explains why $z^2$ doubles angles at the origin: the first nonzero 
derivative is the second.

marginNotes.text: Who determined its measurements—surely you know!

marginNotes.author: Bernhard Riemann
marginNotes.text: The essence of a map lies in what it preserves.
